steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This "find supporting evidence" question asks which finding most directly supports the researchers' conclusion that the transition from a stagnant lid regime to a tectonic plate regime occurred at 3.2 billion years ago. The decisive clue is the mechanism: before the transition, lithosphere and mantle were completely isolated; after it, their material began to mix. Supporting evidence must show mixing beginning at that specific date.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — rock counts shift from more mantle-derived to more lithospheric at 3.2 billion years: The text gives no basis for comparing quantities of rock types; sheer abundance tells us nothing about whether mixing occurred. ✗
  • Choice B — older mantle-derived rocks show more compositional diversity than older lithospheric rocks: Comparing the diversity of two separate rock categories does not show that material transferred between them; this leaves the mixing question unanswered. ✗
  • Choice C — a positive correlation between rock age and chemical similarity to mantle-derived rocks, strengthening at 3.2 billion years: This would mean the oldest lithospheric rocks are the most similar to mantle rocks, contradicting the claim that lithosphere and mantle were isolated before 3.2 billion years ago; the direction is backwards. ✗
  • Choice D — mantle-derived rocks younger than 3.2 billion years contain material found in lithospheric rocks but absent from older mantle-derived rocks: This is the chemical fingerprint of mixing. The foreign material appears in young mantle rocks (after the transition) but not in old ones (before it), directly confirming that lithospheric material began entering the mantle at 3.2 billion years ago. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
